content_en:
Al-Marghinani's treatment of fasting in Al-Hidayah presents the Hanafi school's distinctive positions within the framework of broadly shared Islamic fasting law. The Hanafi school shares the foundational rules of Ramadan fasting with the other schools but differs in several notable respects that reflect its characteristic methodology and its distinct hadith interpretations.

The Ramadan fast is obligatory on every sane, adult Muslim who is resident and physically capable. The Hanafi school holds that the intention for Ramadan fasting may be made at any point before midday — not strictly before Fajr as the Shafi'i and Hanbali schools require. Al-Marghinani justifies this on the grounds that the intent to fast is maintained throughout the night for a Muslim who woke up not having eaten, and the midday threshold represents half the day, before which the act of fasting is still meaningfully intentional. For voluntary fasts, the intention may also be made before midday. However, the Hanafi school agrees that the ideal is to make the intention the night before.

On what breaks the fast, the Hanafi school is notably more expansive than some other schools in identifying nullifiers. Any substance entering the stomach through the mouth, nose, or other natural openings breaks the fast in the Hanafi view, with the exception of substances that are not nourishing and not medicine. However, the Hanafi school's distinctive position is that deliberate vomiting does not break the fast regardless of quantity — only vomiting that returns involuntarily to the stomach constitutes a nullifier. This contrasts with the Shafi'i, Maliki, and Hanbali positions that hold deliberate vomiting of a mouthful or more to break the fast.

The kaffarah (expiation) for deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast through sexual intercourse is: freeing a slave, then fasting sixty consecutive days, then feeding sixty poor people, in a fixed hierarchy. The Hanafi school is the only school to explicitly hold that this kaffarah applies to any deliberate violation of the fast — not only sexual intercourse but also deliberate eating and drinking — based on the narration that the Prophet prescribed kaffarah for the man who had intercourse during Ramadan without specifying that it was limited to that act alone. The other schools restrict kaffarah to sexual intercourse.

For concessions (rukhsah), the Hanafi school permits the sick and the traveler to break the fast, with the sick person having the option — not the obligation — to fast if they are able. The Hanafi school holds that the traveler's permission to break the fast is not conditioned on hardship; the mere fact of travel is sufficient. Pregnant and nursing women who fear harm may break the fast and make up the days, with fidyah (feeding one poor person per day) required as well if the fear was for the child.

Voluntary fasting in the Hanafi school follows the broadly shared Islamic calendar of meritorious fasting days: Mondays and Thursdays, the middle days of each month, the day of Arafah for non-pilgrims, and the ninth and tenth of Muharram. The Hanafi school emphasizes fasting both the ninth and tenth of Muharram (rather than the tenth alone) to distinguish Muslim practice from that of other communities who also fast on the tenth.

Al-Marghinani's fasting chapter exemplifies the Hanafi school's careful engagement with the full range of practical questions that arise in observing the fast — from the traveler who returns home mid-day to the question of whether brushing teeth with a wet toothbrush invalidates the fast.
content_id:
Pembahasan Al-Marghinani mengenai puasa dalam Al-Hidayah menyajikan posisi-posisi khas mazhab Hanafi dalam kerangka hukum puasa Islam yang secara luas dianut bersama. Mazhab Hanafi berbagi ketentuan-ketentuan dasar puasa Ramadan dengan mazhab-mazhab lain, namun berbeda dalam beberapa hal yang mencerminkan metodologi khasnya dan penafsiran hadisnya yang distingtif.

Puasa Ramadan wajib bagi setiap Muslim yang berakal, baligh, bermukim, dan mampu secara fisik. Mazhab Hanafi berpendapat bahwa niat puasa Ramadan boleh dibuat kapan saja sebelum tengah hari — tidak harus sebelum Subuh sebagaimana yang disyaratkan mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanbali. Al-Marghinani membenarkan ini dengan alasan bahwa niat untuk berpuasa terjaga sepanjang malam bagi seorang Muslim yang bangun dalam keadaan belum makan, dan ambang batas tengah hari mewakili setengah hari, sebelum itu amalan puasa masih bermakna secara niat. Untuk puasa sunnah, niat juga boleh dibuat sebelum tengah hari. Namun, mazhab Hanafi sepakat bahwa yang ideal adalah membuat niat pada malam sebelumnya.

Mengenai apa yang membatalkan puasa, mazhab Hanafi cukup lebih luas dari beberapa mazhab lain dalam mengidentifikasi pembatal-pembatal. Setiap zat yang masuk ke lambung melalui mulut, hidung, atau lubang alami lainnya membatalkan puasa menurut pandangan Hanafi, dengan pengecualian zat-zat yang tidak bergizi dan bukan obat-obatan. Namun, posisi khas mazhab Hanafi adalah bahwa muntah yang disengaja tidak membatalkan puasa tanpa memandang jumlahnya — hanya muntah yang kembali ke lambung secara tidak disengaja yang merupakan pembatal. Ini berbeda dari posisi mazhab Syafi'i, Maliki, dan Hanbali yang berpendapat bahwa muntah yang disengaja sebanyak satu mulut atau lebih membatalkan puasa.

Kafarat atas pelanggaran puasa Ramadan yang disengaja melalui hubungan seksual adalah: membebaskan budak, kemudian puasa enam puluh hari berturut-turut, kemudian memberi makan enam puluh orang miskin, dalam hierarki yang bersifat tetap. Mazhab Hanafi adalah satu-satunya mazhab yang secara eksplisit berpendapat bahwa kafarat ini berlaku untuk setiap pelanggaran puasa yang disengaja — tidak hanya hubungan seksual tetapi juga makan dan minum dengan sengaja — berdasarkan riwayat bahwa Nabi ﷺ menetapkan kafarat bagi laki-laki yang berhubungan seksual di siang hari Ramadan tanpa menyebutkan bahwa hal itu terbatas pada perbuatan itu saja. Mazhab-mazhab lain membatasi kafarat hanya pada hubungan seksual.

Untuk rukhsah (keringanan), mazhab Hanafi membolehkan orang sakit dan musafir untuk berbuka puasa, dengan orang sakit memiliki pilihan — bukan kewajiban — untuk tetap berpuasa jika mampu. Mazhab Hanafi berpendapat bahwa izin musafir untuk berbuka puasa tidak disyaratkan adanya kesulitan; fakta perjalanan itu sendiri sudah cukup. Wanita hamil dan menyusui yang khawatir akan bahaya boleh berbuka puasa dan mengqadanya, dengan fidyah (memberi makan satu orang miskin per hari) yang juga diwajibkan jika kekhawatiran itu untuk sang anak.

Puasa sunnah dalam mazhab Hanafi mengikuti kalender Islam yang secara luas dianut mengenai hari-hari puasa yang utama: hari Senin dan Kamis, pertengahan setiap bulan, hari Arafah bagi yang tidak berhaji, serta tanggal sembilan dan sepuluh Muharram. Mazhab Hanafi menekankan puasa pada tanggal sembilan dan sepuluh Muharram (bukan hanya tanggal sepuluh) untuk membedakan praktik Muslim dari komunitas lain yang juga berpuasa pada tanggal sepuluh.

Bab puasa karya Al-Marghinani mencontohkan keterlibatan cermat mazhab Hanafi dengan seluruh spektrum pertanyaan praktis yang muncul dalam menjalankan puasa — mulai dari musafir yang kembali ke rumah di siang hari hingga pertanyaan apakah menggosok gigi dengan sikat basah membatalkan puasa.
content_ar: Al-Marghinani's treatment of الصيام in Al-Hidayah presents the الحنفي school's distinctive positions within the framework of broadly shared Islamic الصيام law. The الحنفي school shares the foundational rules of Ramadan الصيام with the other schools but differs in several notable respects that reflect its characteristic methodology and its distinct الحديث interpretations. The Ramadan fast is obligatory on every sane, adult مسلم who is resident and physically capable. The الحنفي school holds that the intention for Ramadan الصيام may be made at any point قبل midday — not strictly قبل Fajr as the الشافعي and الحنبلي schools require. Al-Marghinani justifies this on the grounds that the intent to fast is maintained throughout the night for a مسلم who woke up not having eaten, and the midday threshold represents half the day, قبل which the act of الصيام is still meaningfully intentional. For voluntary fasts, the intention may أيضاً be made قبل midday. غير أن, the الحنفي school agrees that the ideal is to make the intention the night قبل. On what breaks the fast, the الحنفي school is notably more expansive than some other schools in identifying nullifiers. Any substance entering the stomach through the mouth, nose, or other natural openings breaks the fast in the الحنفي view, with the exception of substances that are not nourishing and not medicine. غير أن, the الحنفي school's distinctive position is that deliberate vomiting does not break the fast regardless of quantity — only vomiting that returns involuntarily to the stomach constitutes a nullifier. This contrasts with the الشافعي, المالكي, and الحنبلي positions that hold deliberate vomiting of a mouthful or more to break the fast. The kaffarah (expiation) for deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast through sexual intercourse is: freeing a slave, then الصيام sixty consecutive days, then feeding sixty poor people, in a fixed hierarchy. The الحنفي school is the only school to explicitly hold that this kaffarah applies to any deliberate violation of the fast — not only sexual intercourse but أيضاً deliberate eating and drinking — based on the narration that النبي ﷺ prescribed kaffarah for the man who had intercourse خلال Ramadan without specifying that it was limited to that act alone. The other schools restrict kaffarah to sexual intercourse. For concessions (rukhsah), the الحنفي school permits the sick and the traveler to break the fast, with the sick person having the option — not the الالتزام — to fast إذا they are able. The الحنفي school holds that the traveler's permission to break the fast is not conditioned on الشدة; the mere fact of travel is sufficient. Pregnant and nursing women who الخوف harm may break the fast and make up the days, with fidyah (feeding one poor person per day) required as well إذا the الخوف wأما the الولد.
